title: Flavio Matos
text:
About

Hi, I’m Flavio. I’m a Data Analytics Consultant with 5+ years of experience helping businesses turn data into better decisions.
My journey started at Unilever, where I worked on process optimization projects that reduced waste and generated over £600,000 in savings. Since then, I’ve partnered with established companies and startups across marketing, finance, and operations to build dashboards, automate reporting, and create scalable analytics solutions.
I enjoy combining technical expertise with business thinking, helping teams move beyond spreadsheets and reports to uncover insights that drive real impact.
Outside of consulting, I’m an active member of a global data community, having trained over 100 professionals, spoken at events across New York, London, Portugal, and Brazil, and received recognitions from Tableau Community for my work and contributions.
My goal is simple: make data more accessible, actionable, and valuable for the people who use it.

Certifications

title: What the F* is Cloud Computing? A Straightforward Introduction
text:
In my previous article, I mentioned that I started my journey towards the AWS Data Engineer certification. If you missed it, check it out here. I shared some tips for the exam and how to prepare for it in just two weeks.
However, before talking more about the certification, let’s step back and answer a simple question:

title: What the F* is Cloud Computing? A Straightforward Introduction
text:
What is cloud computing anyway?
There’s a formal definition of cloud computing wrote by Peter Mell and Timothy Grance published on the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) under the U.S. Department of Commerce (you can find the document here). According to the authors, cloud computing must have 5 essential characteristics. Some of these characteristics are logical and some of them may surprise you. I have attempted to simplify them further down to their core essence to keep this article -less boring- easy to read.

1. On-demand self-service
You can get the computing resources you need, like storage or virtual machines, whenever you want without needing to talk to anyone in advance.

If you can provision your resources whenever you want without involving humans and without delay, It meets this criteria. If not, it’s probably not cloud.



2. Broad network access
You can use cloud services from anywhere using any device with an internet connection, like your phone, tablet, or computer.

If you need to visit a data center or go to visitor’s facility to interact with your data and/or systems, it’s probably not cloud. If you need a private network link to access your environment, it’s probably not cloud.



3. Resource pooling
The cloud provider shares its resources among many users, allocating resources as needed without you knowing exactly where your data is stored.
- This characteristic relates to economies of scale. For instance, you can instruct AWS to deploy a virtual machine in a specific region, and they can use any data center or hardware vendor they choose. This shifts the risk to the vendor (AWS in this example). This is beneficial because it allows businesses to focus on applications, data, and services as their core value, rather than on hardware.

Pooling also facilitates scaling whenever a customer needs it. When you access storage or virtual machines, they come from a pool of resources rather than being dedicated solely to you. Despite sharing the same hardware with other customers, each customer is isolated, ensuring that your data is accessible only to you.



4. Rapid elasticity
You can quickly get more resources or reduce them based on your needs, almost like having an unlimited supply that you can tap into anytime.
- Scaling can occur rapidly in real time with no human interaction. Customers don’t need to worry about overprovisioning or underprovisioning – this is a waste of money. This is how companies like Amazon or Netflix can easily handle holiday sales or the load generated when the latest season of a popular series is released.
- A cloud environment should not let you see capacity limits. If you need a hundred virtual machines or a thousand, you should be able to access them immediately. In the background, the provider manages the capacity in a pooled way, but from your perspective, you should never really see any capacity limitations.

In my opinion, this is THE most important benefit of the cloud: the system scales in size in response to load. It’s crucial to ensure that a potential cloud environment offers this capability to be truly considered a cloud service.



5. Measured service
You only pay for what you use, and you can track your usage. The cloud provider keeps track of how much you use and charges you accordingly.
- Traditional and non-cloud infrastructure worked using CapEx (capital expenditure) model. You paid for servers and hardware upfront, often resulting in excess capacity at the start, leading to wasted money. As demand grew, you would need to purchase more servers. If you scaled up too slowly, you faced performance issues or failures.

A true cloud environment, however, offers on-demand billing. Your usage is continuously monitored, and you pay based on that usage (e.g., pay by time for virtual machines, or pay by gigabytes for storage services). With a genuine cloud platform, you generally pay nothing in advance.

title: What the F* is Cloud Computing? A Straightforward Introduction
section: Why Should I use cloud computing?
text:
Why Should I use cloud computing?
Back in the 90s and 00s, if you didn’t have a PC at home, you would go to an Internet Café to use a computer with internet access for an hourly fee. This works as a good analogy for why you would use cloud computing. It’s essentially a way to rent resources you don’t have, and you pay based on your usage.
Cloud computing offers a plenty of services for individuals and companies. Take AWS, for example. If you want to run a very complex algorithm in R or Python on your computer, it could take hours or even days to complete. By renting a more powerful computer (AWS EC2 Instance) with a better CPU and more RAM, you can run your script waaay faster and only pay for the time you use.
Need a larger drive to back up your holiday photos or store data from your website? AWS’s S3 service allows you to do that and pay only for the storage you use.
For companies, the benefits are even more significant. You can migrate your entire data center to a cloud provider like AWS, which can reduce costs while improving security and availability. For example, Netflix uses cloud computing services to store and distribute movies globally, ensuring that a client in Brazil and another in Japan can watch with minimal latency.

In summary, you would use cloud computing to “rent” superior computer resources, improving performance, security, and cost efficiency for your scripts, business, and operations.

title: What the F* is Cloud Computing? A Straightforward Introduction
text:
Who are the cloud providers?
The most popular cloud computing services providers are Amazon Web Services (AWS) , Microsoft Azure , and Google Cloud . They offer similar services and products through their platforms, including virtual machines, storage services, and various tools to help businesses maximize the value of their data. These tools enable data analysis and leverage machine learning and AI to gain a competitive edge.
In the next article, we will discuss what businesses need to do to reach the level where they can effectively use machine learning and AI. You will also be able to self-diagnose to determine where your business currently stands in the data journey and identify the steps needed to achieve AI capabilities.

title: Dev Spotlight: Translating Dashboards for a Global Audience
text:
About the event
Join us for the very first Dev Spotlight session at the Lisbon Tableau User Group!
Have you ever thought about bringing your insights to a wider, global audience?
In this session, I will demonstrate a practical way to translate dashboards and make your visualizations accessible to people all over the world. Together, we will explore how your data can “speak” different languages to amplify its impact. Of course, we will have a live Tableau demonstration so you can start applying these techniques right away!
🚀 Don’t miss this opportunity to learn and exchange experiences with the community!
Please note: This event will be conducted in Portuguese.


Recording





 Back to top

title: Tableau and Slack Integration
text:
In this session, I demonstrate how to connect Tableau Server to a Slack workspace to streamline communication and data collaboration.
I share practical examples of how to:
- Automate Updates: Deliver regular dashboard snapshots directly to Slack channels, replacing traditional email reports.
- Real-time Monitoring: Configure automatic Slack alerts to track specific metrics instantly.
- Drive Efficiency: Ensure your team has fast, integrated access to relevant data for quicker decision-making.
Join us to transform how you and your team work with data!
Please note: This event will be conducted in Portuguese.

title: How to empower your Nonprofit organisation with Tableau’s data insights
text:
This session will explore the transformative power of data for the nonprofit sector (NGOs and NPOs). Despite data being an invaluable asset, many organizations struggle to harness its potential due to limited tools and resources.
Key topics covered include the impact of visual analytics on:

Strategic Fundraising: Identifying donor trends and campaign opportunities.
Impact Reporting: Transparently communicating results to stakeholders.
Operational Efficiency: Monitoring daily activities to maximize mission impact.

The event features a case study on REACH, a Cambodian charity using these insights to provide life-changing education and vocational training.

title: How to pass the AWS Cloud Practitioner exam in 2 weeks
text:
If you’ve come across this post, chances are you’re considering taking the AWS Cloud Practitioner certification or you’ve already done so. This marks the beginning of my journey towards the AWS Data Engineer Certification, which is my ultimate goal. Every journey starts with a single step, and for me, it’s the AWS Cloud Practitioner certification.
The idea of getting the AWS Cloud Practitioner certification began with a simple desire to understand more about cloud computing. It all started when we had a session on AWS Cloud Services at the The Data School UK . Knowing the importance of cloud services like Amazon Web Services (AWS) , Microsoft Azure , or Google Cloud for future clients, I was eager to explore more the subject.

title: How to pass the AWS Cloud Practitioner exam in 2 weeks
text:
The Exam
The AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner certification is a great starting point for anyone beginning their journey into AWS. It validates a foundational, high-level understanding of AWS Cloud, services, and terminology.
The exam consists of 65 questions, which can be either multiple-choice or multiple-response format. You’ll have 90 minutes to answer all the questions, and the minimum passing score is 700 (in a scale 100 – 1.000)
You have the option to take the exam either in-person at a Pearson VUE testing center or through an online proctored exam. Additionally, the exam is available in several languages, including English, Chinese, Spanish, French, German, Italian, and Portuguese (Brazil).
Once you pass the exam, your certification will be valid for 3 years.

title: How to pass the AWS Cloud Practitioner exam in 2 weeks
section: How to prepare to the exam
text:
How to prepare to the exam
I found out about the AWS Skills Builder website and decided to binge-watch the AWS Cloud Practitioner Essentials training course. The course provided a solid foundation in cloud computing basics, with easy-to-follow videos and practical examples. Their use of the coffee shop analogy to explain all the services and solutions made complex concepts easy to grasp.
I completed the training in the first week, and as I neared the end, I focused on preparing for the exam. The last module of the training is focused on the exam and includes a quiz to test your readiness. I took the quiz three times because I wanted to score more than 90% to ensure I was fully prepared.

Tip: For each quiz question, I tried to select the correct answer and explain why the other alternatives were incorrect.

I also discovered a “podcast” called AWS PartnerCast, which provides tips for the exam and highlights important topics. This helped me identify key study areas and offered insights on exam settings such as time management and question types, giving me a better understanding of what to expect during the exam.
So, two weeks after starting my journey towards AWS Cloud Practitioner certification, I successfully passed the exam.

title: How to pass the AWS Cloud Practitioner exam in 2 weeks
section: Do I need to have any practical experience?
text:
Do I need to have any practical experience?
With just 2 weeks of preparation and no prior practical experience, it’s totally possible to pass the exam. However, it’s important to note that it doesn’t mean you have comprehensive knowledge of AWS cloud computing.
The certification validates foundational, high-level understanding of AWS Cloud, its services, and terminology. It’s a good starting point on the AWS journey.
Now that I have my certification, I requested to join projects for clients who use AWS to gain more hands-on experience. In one of these projects, I got to use CloudWatch, Athena, and S3. These were all tools that I had learned about during my preparation, and now I’m diving even deeper into understanding their functionalities and applications.

title: How to pass the AWS Cloud Practitioner exam in 2 weeks
text:
Next Steps
I’m excited to continue my journey in cloud computing. My next goal is to pursue the AWS Solutions Architect certification, building on the knowledge I’ve gained.
I’m thinking to share my experiences and insights along the way, and perhaps will be posting updates on my blog. Whether you’re considering certification or have already taken the exam, I’d love to hear from you any tips, suggestions and questions you might have.

📱Social media
You can find me on LinkedIn and Twitter
Check out my portfolio on Tableau Public

title: Slack & Tableau Integration
text:
Connecting Tableau Server with Slack can transform the way you and your team work, making it easier to share information and collaborate directly within Slack.
Some of the benefits of this integration include:
• Setup Alerts: Monitor KPIs directly in Slack.
• Real-Time Collaboration: Share insights for instant discussion.
• Faster Decision-Making: Access critical data without even opening Tableau Server.
Before starting this tutorial, it is important to remember that connecting Tableau Server to Slack requires creating a Slack application first. It may seem complicated, but by following the step-by-step instructions below, you will see that it is quite simple.

title: Slack & Tableau Integration
text:
The Exam

Once you pass the exam, your certification will be valid for 3 years.

I’m thinking to share my experiences and insights along the way, and perhaps will be posting updates on my blog. Whether you’re considering certification or have already taken the exam, I’d love to hear from you any tips, suggestions and questions you might have.

title: Slack & Tableau Integration
section: How to Create a Slack App
text:
How to Create a Slack App
1. Go to the Slack API documentation, click Create New App, and select the From Scratch option.











2. Choose a name for the app, select a workspace, and click Create App.






3. In the side menu, go to App Home and click Review Scopes to Add.






4. Scroll down to the Scopes section and, under Bot Token Scopes, click Add an OAuth Scope. Then select the following permissions:
   • chat:write
   • files:write
   • users:read
   • users:read.email







5. In the side menu, select OAuth & Permissions and scroll down to the Redirect URL section.
   Click Add New Redirect URL and enter the URL of the Tableau Server you want to connect, using the following format: https://<Tableau Server URL>/auth/add_oauth_token






6. Select Basic Information from the side menu and copy or save the following details:
   • Client ID
   • Client Secret
   • Redirect URL entered in the previous step

⚠️ You will need this information shortly.






7. Click Install App and then the green Install to <Your Workspace> button.
Slack will ask for permission to allow the app to access your company workspace. If you agree, click Allow.












Done! You will see a message confirming that the app has been installed successfully.

Now we need to connect Tableau Server to the app we just created.

title: Slack & Tableau Integration
text:
Connecting Tableau Server to Slack

1. In Tableau Server, click Settings in the side menu and then select the Integrations tab.






2. Under Slack Connectivity, click Add OAuth Client and enter the information you copied in step 6:
   • Client ID
   • Client Secret
   • Redirect URL

Then click Add OAuth Client.






3. You will see the Connection Type and Client ID listed in the table below. Click Connect to Slack.

A new window will appear asking you to approve the permissions required to connect Tableau Server to Slack. If you agree, click Allow.










Done! Your Tableau Slack App is now created and connected to Tableau Server.

title: Slack & Tableau Integration
text:
Bonus: Testing the Integration

1. Open Slack, click Add Apps, and select the app we just created (in this example, Tableau Server App).











2. To test the integration, open any view in Tableau Server and click Share.
Select yourself as the recipient and add a message (optional).

⚠️ Important: The email address used in Tableau Server must be the same as the one registered in Slack.






3. You will receive a Slack notification informing you that you have a message from the Tableau App.





And that concludes our tutorial on how to integrate Tableau Server with Slack!

I hope you found this guide useful. Let me know in the comments if it worked for you, and if you have any questions, feel free to leave a comment!



📱Social media
You can find me on LinkedIn and Twitter
Check out my portfolio on Tableau Public

title: Let’s play with colours
text:
How many of you have seen a chart with green for profit, and red for loss? I’m definitely in that group!
The concept is so familiar and widespread that we rarely stop to think (or want to spend time thinking) about colour. But colour can be your greatest ally or your worst enemy.
So, here’s a guide that will help you use colour smartly, without spending hours agonising over it.
Let’s start with the obvious question:

title: Let’s play with colours
section: Let’s start with the obvious question: why use colour at all?
text:
Let’s start with the obvious question: why use colour at all?
The most direct answer is that colours help us identify differences quickly and easily. Our eyes are wired to spot contrasts by colour almost instantly. So, when a CFO glances at your dashboard, it takes them less than 2 seconds to understand whether the result is good or bad, and what action to take next.
A quick (and fun… well, kind of fun) way to see this in action is to compare how easily you can spot how many number 9s there are in the image. Notice how effortless it is to identify 9s through colour contrast, highlighting only the information that matter.


Great! Now we know why it’s important. Let’s focus on how to use it. Here are some best practices that, if you follow them, will put you ahead of 95% of people who just slap red/green on everything.
Quick note: At the end of this article I’ll show you exactly why you MUST avoid using green/red in your reports.

📱Social media
You can find me on LinkedIn and Twitter
Check out my portfolio on Tableau Public

title: Let’s play with colours
text:
1. Be Consistent
The first rule of thumb: be consistent with your colours. If you’re building a report showing numbers for Apples and Oranges, stick to the same colours for each category throughout the entire report.

title: Let’s play with colours
text:
2. Use Brand Guidelines
You don’t need to reinvent the wheel. You can (and sometimes must) use brand guidelines and colour palettes. This is a huge time-saver (a lifesaver too).
Following a brand’s colour palette makes your reports and dashboards feel familiar, gives them a brand identity, and saves you from making an embarrassing mistake.
I doubt that red means something negative for Coca-Cola, Netflix or China!
Starbucks 2026 Annual Report





Premier League Annual Report 2024/25

title: Let’s play with colours
section: 3. Use Colours Sparingly… Unless It’s Carnival
text:
3. Use Colours Sparingly… Unless It’s Carnival
Don’t crowd your dashboards with 12+ different colours. Use them sparingly.
The worst data visualisation you can create is one where every value or category gets its own colour. That’s just colourful, not insightful.
Colour should guide attention and highlight what matters. Everything else can stay neutral.

title: Let’s play with colours
section: 4. Use Colour with Intention… But I Hope Your Intentions Are Good!
text:
4. Use Colour with Intention… But I Hope Your Intentions Are Good!
This tip is gold, and incredibly powerful. You can take a single chart and tell 3 completely different stories, just by highlighting different elements. Being intentional with colour is a way of telling your audience what matters most.
But, as Uncle Ben told Spider-Man Peter Parker: with great power comes great responsibility.
You can draw attention to exactly what you want to emphasize, which also means you can guide your audience toward a particular conclusion. Use that power wisely. You’re a data warrior, not a data villain!
In the example below, the authors wanted to draw attention to the fact that 48-month lease units decreased by 36%. However, you can notice that 12-month lease units decreased a bit less, by about 26%, while cash purchases still grew by 10%.
The way you present the data depends on the story you want to tell and what is most relevant to your audience. This should guide where you choose to focus their attention.





Great! Now you have 4 golden tips to use in your dashboards! Tips that will help you stand out from 95% of other dashboards, save time picking colours, and prove your point by being intentional.
Now let’s talk about what colours to use.

title: Let’s play with colours
text:
5. Colour Palette types
There are essentially 3 types of colour palette you need to know when building your reports: sequential, divergent, and discrete.

A. Sequential
Use a sequential colour palette for continuous data, things like temperature, distance, height, weight, and so on. Because the data is continuous, the colours should be too, and we typically use gradients to represent that continuity.







B. Divergent
Still within continuous data, divergent palettes work best when you need to show contrast or when there’s a meaningful midpoint.
They’re great for displaying negative and positive numbers, with zero as the centre or for showing variations around a median.
Temperature (in Celsius) is a classic use case, as is any metric where the middle point is just as meaningful as the extremes.







C. Discrete
Discrete colour palettes are for categorical data, dimensions and groups that represent fundamentally different things. The goal here is to help your audience immediately see what belongs to what.
Discrete palettes work in almost any chart type: pie charts (each slice gets its own colour), donut charts, bar charts, line charts with multiple series, and even binary cases like achieved/not achieved, true/false, in/out.

But wait…here’s the trap! Discrete palettes are incredibly versatile, but this is also where most people forget the “use colours sparingly” rule.
When you have more than 6 categories and assign a different colour to each one, your dashboard starts to look like a Carnival poster or a painting from MoMA.
You do not want to be that person.
You do not want to be that person. (I wrote that twice so it really sinks in).
Ok, but, what if I’m building a chart with all the countries in the world, nearly 200 of them? Great question!
You can easily group them: by the 5 continents, OECD membership, BRICS, or any other meaningful grouping. Here is an examples of using discrete colours sparingly even with many dimensions:

title: Let’s play with colours
text:
One More Thing: Accessibility
Now you know everything about colour palettes, but before you head to LinkedIn to announce that you’re the Van Gogh of dataviz, we need to talk about accessibility.
When building reports and dashboards, you need to ensure that everyone can access your data and read your insights. That means choosing colours that are colour-blind friendly, where the contrast between colours is still clear to people with colour vision deficiencies. And yes, this is exactly why you must stop using green/red.


Tableau has a colour-blind friendly palette built in, but you can also check your own palette using these free tools:

Pilestone
Color Oracle
RGBlind
Coblis

Building your dashboards with your audience in mind not only makes you more intentional with colour choices, it also helps your ideas reach more people. More people will be able to see your insights and contribute to the conversation.

title: Let’s play with colours
section: Steal like an artist! - Inspirations and References
text:
Steal like an artist! - Inspirations and References
Looking for colour palette inspiration? Here are some great places to start:
Brand’s website and annual reports
Big companies pay agencies millions to craft their colour guidelines, and you can use those for free.
No need to reinvent the wheel.
Pinterest and Google Images
Try searching for a product category (like laundry detergents) and notice how the results are dominated by blues and light blues.
We’re already subconsciously familiar with colour associations, and you can leverage that to add context to your dataviz.
The real world
Go outside! Take a walk around the block. Notice the colours around you in everyday life.
You’ll find that everything follows a colour pattern. Feel free to get inspired .
The Adobe Colour Wheel
If you want to create your own palette from scratch, Adobe’s Colour Wheel helps you pick combinations that actually work together, based on colour theory.

Adobe also has a fantastic tool that extracts a colour theme directly from an image. If you want to use a painting (say, a Van Gogh) but aren’t sure which colours dominate or what the hex codes are, this tool does the work for you. I strongly recommend giving it a try.
So now you know how to use colour in your data visualisations.
You won’t waste hours on it anymore, because you know there are plenty of ready-to-go references to steal out there, and you know the best practices to follow to make sure your dashboards look polished (not like a primary school art project).
Go build your next dataviz! But keep this article handy to double-check you’re following all the tips. Your audience will thank you for it.
And most importantly…please, stop using green and red. For good.


📱Social media
You can find me on LinkedIn and Twitter
Check out my portfolio on Tableau Public

title: Let’s play with colours
text:
Why use colour at all?
The most direct answer is that colours help us identify differences quickly and easily. Our eyes are wired to spot contrasts by colour almost instantly.
So, when a CFO glances at your dashboard, it takes them less than a second to understand whether the result is good or bad, and what action to take next.
A quick (and fun… well, kind of fun) way to see this in action is to compare how easily you can spot how many number 9s there are in the image.
Notice how effortless it is to identify 9s through colour contrast, highlighting only the information that matter.










Great! Now we know why it’s important. Let’s focus on how to use it. Here are some best practices that, if you follow them, will put you ahead of 95% of people who just slap red/green on everything.

Quick note: At the end of this article I’ll show you exactly why you should avoid using green/red in your reports.

title: Let’s play with colours
text:
One Last Thing: Accessibility
Now you know everything about colour palettes, but before you head to LinkedIn to announce that you’re the Van Gogh of dataviz, we need to talk about accessibility.
When building reports and dashboards, you need to ensure that everyone can access your data and read your insights.
That means choosing colours that are colour-blind friendly, where the contrast between colours is still clear to people with colour vision deficiencies. And yes, this is exactly why you should stop using green/red.











Example of how someone with colour blindness (deuteranopia) would see the chart using red and green colours.

Tableau has a colour-blind friendly palette built in, but you can also check your own palette using these free tools:

Pilestone
Color Oracle
RGBlind
Coblis

Building your dashboards with your audience in mind not only makes you more intentional with colour choices, it also helps your ideas reach more people.
More people will be able to see your insights and contribute to the conversation.
